explanation_md: `a{}` uses both defaults so `width=10` and `height=5`. `b{20}` supplies `20` for the first member (`width`), overriding its default, but `height` gets no value so it falls back to its default of `5`. The default is only bypassed for the members that are explicitly provided.

options.a: `std::string` cannot have a default member initializer.
options.b: `score` has no default member initializer, so it is value-initialized to `0`, not `100`.
options.c: Using `{}` on a struct with mixed-initialized members is ill-formed.
options.d: The struct definition is missing a semicolon.

explanation_md: `score` has no default member initializer, so with `Player p{}` it is value-initialized to `0` (not `100`). The fix is to write `int score{ 100 };` in the struct definition. Option `a` is wrong — `std::string` can absolutely have a default initializer.

prompt_md: Which statement best describes what happens when aggregate initialization provides a value for a member that also has a default member initializer?

options.b: The default initializer value is used and the provided value is ignored.
options.c: The provided value is used and the default initializer is ignored.
options.d: The program fails to compile because of the conflict.

explanation_md: The explicitly provided value always wins. Default member initializers only take effect when no value is given for that member during aggregate initialization. There is no conflict and no combining — the default is simply skipped.

prompt_md:
Consider `struct S { int x; };`. Which initialization leaves `x` with undefined behavior on a subsequent read?

```cpp
S a{};   // (1)
S b{ 5 }; // (2)
S c;     // (3)
```
explanation_md: `S c;` (no braces, no default initializer) leaves `x` uninitialized; reading it is undefined behavior. `S a{}` value-initializes `x` to `0`, and `S b{5}` explicitly sets `x` to `5`. The absence of braces is the hazard when no default initializer is present.
